question:
5. Let \( a_{j}(j=1,2, \ldots, n) \) be entirely arbitrary numbers except that no one is equal to unity. Prove
\[
\left.a_{1}+\sum_{i=2}^{n} a_{i} \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}\left(1-a_{j}\right)=1-\prod_{j=1}^{n}\left(1-a_{j}\right) \quad \quad \text { (page } 350\right)
\]
solution:
Solution. The given statement is true for \( n=1 \) (interpreting the empty sum as 0 ) and for \( n=2 \). Suppose it is true for \( n=k \), i.e.,
\[
a_{1}+\sum_{i=2}^{k} a_{i} \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}\left(1-a_{j}\right)=1-\prod_{i=1}^{k}\left(1-a_{i}\right)
\]

Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
a_{1}+\sum_{i=2}^{k+1} a_{i} \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}\left(1-a_{j}\right) & =a_{1}+\sum_{i=2}^{k} a_{i} \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}\left(1-a_{j}\right)+a_{k+1} \prod_{j=1}^{k}\left(1-a_{j}\right) \\
& =1-\prod_{i=1}^{k}\left(1-a_{i}\right)+a_{k+1} \prod_{j=1}^{k}\left(1-a_{j}\right) \\
& =1-\left|\prod_{i=1}^{k}\left(1-a_{i}\right)\right|\left(1-a_{k+1}\right) \\
& =1-\prod_{i=1}^{k+1}\left(1-a_{i}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the statement is true for \( n=k+1 \). It follows by induction that it is true for all positive integers \( n \).

Remark. It is not necessary to require that none of the \( a \) 's be unity.

descriptive_long.question:
5. Let \( sequenceelemj(indexnext=1,2, \ldots, totalcount) \) be entirely arbitrary numbers except that no one is equal to unity. Prove
\[
\left.sequenceelemone+\sum_{indexchar=2}^{totalcount} sequenceelemi \prod_{indexnext=1}^{indexchar-1}\left(1-sequenceelemj\right)=1-\prod_{indexnext=1}^{totalcount}\left(1-sequenceelemj\right) \quad \quad \text { (page } 350\right)
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The given statement is true for \( totalcount=1 \) (interpreting the empty sum as 0 ) and for \( totalcount=2 \). Suppose it is true for \( totalcount=indexthird \), i.e.,
\[
sequenceelemone+\sum_{indexchar=2}^{indexthird} sequenceelemi \prod_{indexnext=1}^{indexchar-1}\left(1-sequenceelemj\right)=1-\prod_{indexchar=1}^{indexthird}\left(1-sequenceelemi\right)
\]

Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
sequenceelemone+\sum_{indexchar=2}^{indexthird+1} sequenceelemi \prod_{indexnext=1}^{indexchar-1}\left(1-sequenceelemj\right) & =sequenceelemone+\sum_{indexchar=2}^{indexthird} sequenceelemi \prod_{indexnext=1}^{indexchar-1}\left(1-sequenceelemj\right)+sequenceelemkplus \prod_{indexnext=1}^{indexthird}\left(1-sequenceelemj\right) \\
& =1-\prod_{indexchar=1}^{indexthird}\left(1-sequenceelemi\right)+sequenceelemkplus \prod_{indexnext=1}^{indexthird}\left(1-sequenceelemj\right) \\
& =1-\left|\prod_{indexchar=1}^{indexthird}\left(1-sequenceelemi\right)\right|\left(1-sequenceelemkplus\right) \\
& =1-\prod_{indexchar=1}^{indexthird+1}\left(1-sequenceelemi\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the statement is true for \( totalcount=indexthird+1 \). It follows by induction that it is true for all positive integers \( totalcount \).

Remark. It is not necessary to require that none of the sequence elements be unity.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Let \( asteroid (horseshoe=1,2, \ldots, buttercup) \) be entirely arbitrary numbers except that no one is equal to unity. Prove
\[
honeycomb+\sum_{lighthouse=2}^{buttercup} driftwood \prod_{horseshoe=1}^{lighthouse-1}\left(1-asteroid\right)=1-\prod_{horseshoe=1}^{buttercup}\left(1-asteroid\right) \quad \quad \text { (page } 350\right)
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The given statement is true for \( buttercup=1 \) (interpreting the empty sum as 0 ) and for \( buttercup=2 \). Suppose it is true for \( buttercup=snowflake \), i.e.,
\[
honeycomb+\sum_{lighthouse=2}^{snowflake} driftwood \prod_{horseshoe=1}^{lighthouse-1}\left(1-asteroid\right)=1-\prod_{lighthouse=1}^{snowflake}\left(1-driftwood\right)
\]

Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
honeycomb+\sum_{lighthouse=2}^{snowflake+1} driftwood \prod_{horseshoe=1}^{lighthouse-1}\left(1-asteroid\right) & =honeycomb+\sum_{lighthouse=2}^{snowflake} driftwood \prod_{horseshoe=1}^{lighthouse-1}\left(1-asteroid\right)+tangerine \prod_{horseshoe=1}^{snowflake}\left(1-asteroid\right) \\
& =1-\prod_{lighthouse=1}^{snowflake}\left(1-driftwood\right)+tangerine \prod_{horseshoe=1}^{snowflake}\left(1-asteroid\right) \\
& =1-\left|\prod_{lighthouse=1}^{snowflake}\left(1-driftwood\right)\right|\left(1-tangerine\right) \\
& =1-\prod_{lighthouse=1}^{snowflake+1}\left(1-driftwood\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the statement is true for \( buttercup=snowflake+1 \). It follows by induction that it is true for all positive integers \( buttercup \).

Remark. It is not necessary to require that none of the \( a \)'s be unity.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. Let \( unityconstant(totalextent=1,2, \ldots, singularcount) \) be entirely arbitrary numbers except that no one is equal to unity. Prove
\[
nullityscalar+\sum_{holisticunit=2}^{singularcount} identityfigure \prod_{totalextent=1}^{holisticunit-1}\left(1-unityconstant\right)=1-\prod_{totalextent=1}^{singularcount}\left(1-unityconstant\right) \quad \quad \text { (page } 350\right)
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The given statement is true for \( singularcount=1 \) (interpreting the empty sum as 0 ) and for \( singularcount=2 \). Suppose it is true for \( singularcount=aggregatepoint \), i.e.,
\[
nullityscalar+\sum_{holisticunit=2}^{aggregatepoint} identityfigure \prod_{totalextent=1}^{holisticunit-1}\left(1-unityconstant\right)=1-\prod_{holisticunit=1}^{aggregatepoint}\left(1-identityfigure\right)
\]

Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
nullityscalar+\sum_{holisticunit=2}^{aggregatepoint+1} identityfigure \prod_{totalextent=1}^{holisticunit-1}\left(1-unityconstant\right) & =nullityscalar+\sum_{holisticunit=2}^{aggregatepoint} identityfigure \prod_{totalextent=1}^{holisticunit-1}\left(1-unityconstant\right)+continuityaspect \prod_{totalextent=1}^{aggregatepoint}\left(1-unityconstant\right) \\ & =1-\prod_{holisticunit=1}^{aggregatepoint}\left(1-identityfigure\right)+continuityaspect \prod_{totalextent=1}^{aggregatepoint}\left(1-unityconstant\right) \\ & =1-\left|\prod_{holisticunit=1}^{aggregatepoint}\left(1-identityfigure\right)\right|\left(1-continuityaspect\right) \\ & =1-\prod_{holisticunit=1}^{aggregatepoint+1}\left(1-identityfigure\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the statement is true for \( singularcount=aggregatepoint+1 \). It follows by induction that it is true for all positive integers \( singularcount \).

Remark. It is not necessary to require that none of the unityconstant 's be unity.

garbled_string.question:
5. Let \( qveropli(snerqtuv=1,2, \ldots, fradomix) \) be entirely arbitrary numbers except that no one is equal to unity. Prove
\[
\left.rogdispa+\sum_{quxbadly=2}^{fradomix} klumseta \prod_{snerqtuv=1}^{quxbadly-1}\left(1-qveropli\right)=1-\prod_{snerqtuv=1}^{fradomix}\left(1-qveropli\right) \quad \quad \text { (page } 350\right)
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The given statement is true for \( fradomix=1 \) (interpreting the empty sum as 0 ) and for \( fradomix=2 \). Suppose it is true for \( fradomix=plimztrq \), i.e.,
\[
rogdispa+\sum_{quxbadly=2}^{plimztrq} klumseta \prod_{snerqtuv=1}^{quxbadly-1}\left(1-qveropli\right)=1-\prod_{quxbadly=1}^{plimztrq}\left(1-klumseta\right)
\]

Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
rogdispa+\sum_{quxbadly=2}^{plimztrq+1} klumseta \prod_{snerqtuv=1}^{quxbadly-1}\left(1-qveropli\right) & =rogdispa+\sum_{quxbadly=2}^{plimztrq} klumseta \prod_{snerqtuv=1}^{quxbadly-1}\left(1-qveropli\right)+zlotimex \prod_{snerqtuv=1}^{plimztrq}\left(1-qveropli\right) \\
& =1-\prod_{quxbadly=1}^{plimztrq}\left(1-klumseta\right)+zlotimex \prod_{snerqtuv=1}^{plimztrq}\left(1-qveropli\right) \\
& =1-\left|\prod_{quxbadly=1}^{plimztrq}\left(1-klumseta\right)\right|\left(1-zlotimex\right) \\
& =1-\prod_{quxbadly=1}^{plimztrq+1}\left(1-klumseta\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the statement is true for \( fradomix=plimztrq+1 \). It follows by induction that it is true for all positive integers \( fradomix \).

Remark. It is not necessary to require that none of the \( qveropli \)'s be unity.

kernel_variant.question:
Let R be an associative ring with identity 1 and let a_1,\ldots ,a_n\in R be idempotents (a_j^2=a_j).  
Fix once and for all the natural order 1<2<\cdots <n.  For every finite set S\subseteq {1,\ldots ,n} write  
S= {i_1<\cdots <i_k}.  Define inductively

  b_\emptyset  := 0,  
  b_{S\cup {i}} := b_S + (1-b_S) a_i (1-b_S)        (i\notin S).                                       (\star )  

(A)  (Algebraic structure)  
(i) Prove that every b_S is an idempotent.  
(ii) Show that if all a_j commute pair-wise, then for every S one has  
  b_S = 1 - \prod _{j\in S}(1-a_j).  

(B)  (Order sensitivity)  
Construct 2\times 2-matrix idempotents a_1,a_2 over \mathbb{Q} for which b_{ {1,2} }\neq b_{ {2,1} }, thereby showing that the conclusion of (A)(ii) fails without commutativity.

(C)  (Orthogonal projections)  
Let H be a Hilbert space and let a_j=P_{H_j} be the orthogonal projection onto a closed subspace H_j\subset H.  
(i)  Interpret b_S as the orthogonal projection onto the closed linear span of (H_j)_{j\in S}.  
(ii)  Give an explicit closed formula for P_{H_1+\cdots +H_n} that involves only alternating sums of products of the individual projections and prove that every numerical coefficient appearing in that formula has operator norm 1.

(D)  (Probabilistic by-product)  
Let (\Omega ,F,P) be a probability space and set a_j:=1_{E_j} for events E_j.  Assume only pair-wise independence (no higher-order independence).  
(i)  Show that the random idempotent b_{ {1,\ldots ,n} } obtained from (\star ) satisfies  

  P(\bigcup _{j=1}^{n}E_j)=E[b_{ {1,\ldots ,n} }].  

(ii)  Compute explicitly the correction term that distinguishes this identity from the classical inclusion-exclusion formula and show that all mixed moments of order \geq 3 cancel automatically.

(E)  (Algorithmic complexity)  
Design an algorithm that, given n symbolic idempotents, outputs b_{ {1,\ldots ,n} } in time \Theta (n\cdot 2^n) measured in ring multiplications, and prove that---even when R is the free ring on the generators a_1,\ldots ,a_n---no algorithm can do better than 2^{\Omega (n)} ring operations.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we abbreviate u_S := 1-b_S and note that u_S is an idempotent whenever b_S is.

(A)  Algebraic structure  
(i)  Idempotency of the b_S.  
Induction over |S|.  The empty set case is trivial.  
Assume b_S^2=b_S.  Put b':=b_S and define b'' by (\star ):  

 b''=b'+(1-b')a_i(1-b')=b'+u'a_i u'  (u':=1-b').  

Compute b''^2:  

 b''^2= (b'+u'a_i u')^2  
      = b'^2 + b'u'a_i u' + u'a_i u'b' + (u'a_i u')^2.  

Because b'u'=u'b'=0 and u'^2=u', the middle two summands vanish.  
Since a_i^2=a_i and u'^2=u',  

 (u'a_i u')^2 = u'a_i u'a_i u' = u'a_i u'.  

Hence b''^2 = b' + u'a_i u' = b''.  Thus b'' is idempotent, completing the induction.

(ii)  Commuting case.  
Assume a_i a_j = a_j a_i for all i,j.  Then u_S commutes with every a_k, and  

 b_{S\cup {i}} = b_S + u_S a_i = 1 - u_S + u_S(1-(1-a_i)) = 1 - u_S(1-a_i).  

With u_\emptyset =1 and u_{S\cup {i}} = u_S(1-a_i) we obtain by induction  

 u_S = \prod _{j\in S}(1-a_j),  whence b_S = 1 - \prod _{j\in S}(1-a_j).

(B)  Order sensitivity: a concrete counter-example.  
Take  

 a_1 = [1 0;0 0],  a_2 = [1 1;0 0] \in  M_2(\mathbb{Q}).  

Both satisfy a_j^2=a_j.  A direct computation yields  

 b_{ {1,2} } = a_1 + (1-a_1)a_2(1-a_1) = [1 0;0 0],  

while  

 b_{ {2,1} } = a_2 + (1-a_2)a_1(1-a_2) = [1 \frac{1}{2};0 0].  

Hence the order matters when the a_j fail to commute.

(C)  Orthogonal projections  
Because projections are idempotent self-adjoint operators, the operator defined by (\star ) is again a projection and satisfies Ran b_{S\cup {i}} = Ran b_S + H_i.  Induction on |S| gives Ran b_S = \Sigma _{j\in S} H_j, so b_{ {1,\ldots ,n} } is the orthogonal projection onto the closed linear span.  

To obtain an explicit formula note that  

 u_{S\cup {i}} = (1-a_i)u_S(1-a_i).  

Start with u_\emptyset =1 and iterate:  

 u_{ {1,\ldots ,n} } = (1-a_n)\ldots (1-a_1)\ldots (1-a_n).                        (\dagger )  

Therefore  

 P_{H_1+\cdots +H_n}=1-u_{ {1,\ldots ,n} }=1-(1-a_n)\ldots (1-a_1)\ldots (1-a_n).  

On expanding (\dagger ) every monomial appears with coefficient \pm 1, hence the numerical coefficients have modulus 1; because projections have operator norm 1, every coefficient in norm is also 1.

(D)  Probabilistic by-product  
The characteristic functions a_j commute (they are functions on \Omega ), so part (A)(ii) gives  

 b_{ {1,\ldots ,n} } = 1 - \prod _{j=1}^{n}(1-1_{E_j}).  

Taking expectation we obtain  

 P(\bigcup E_j)=1-E[\prod (1-1_{E_j})].  

Expand the product:  

 \prod (1-1_{E_j}) = 1 - \Sigma 1_{E_j} + \Sigma _{i<j}1_{E_i}1_{E_j} - \cdots  .  

Pairwise independence implies E[1_{E_i}1_{E_j}]=P(E_i)P(E_j).  Every mixed moment of order \geq 3 equals P(E_{i_1})\cdots P(E_{i_k}) only when full independence is available; but in the expectation of b_{ {1,\ldots ,n} } those higher-order terms cancel with the alternating signs.  One finds  

 P(\bigcup E_j) = \Sigma  P(E_j) - \Sigma _{i<j}P(E_i)P(E_j).             (\ddagger )  

The second summation is the desired quadratic correction; (\ddagger ) coincides with the classical inclusion-exclusion formula when the events are fully independent, because then P(E_i\cap E_j)=P(E_i)P(E_j) and the quadratic term cancels.

(E)  Algorithmic complexity  
Straight expansion of (\dagger ) contains 2^n distinct words in the generators, and no two of them cancel in the free ring.  Thus any algorithm that outputs b_{ {1,\ldots ,n} } in that ring must at some point create all 2^n monomials, whence it needs at least 2^{\Omega (n)} multiplications.  

Conversely, begin with the set {1}.  Replacing u by (1-a_i)u(1-a_i) multiplies every current word first on the left and then on the right by (1-a_i), doubling their number; keeping words in a hash-table gives \Theta (|current set|) ring multiplications per step.  After the i-th step exactly 2I words are present, so the total cost is \Sigma _{i=1}^{n}2I = \Theta (n\cdot 2^n).  Hence \Theta (n\cdot 2^n) is optimal up to a polynomial factor.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
